name: The Lever (as a System)
tagline: A rigid bar, pivot, load, and effort — mechanical advantage for lifting, holding, and precise force application
problem:
Human muscle output is limited and joints fatigue. Many tasks require forces beyond comfortable grip: lifting stone blocks, extracting roots, holding a split log while binding it, or applying controlled pressure to flake stone. The lever problem is redirecting and amplifying force through a rigid body rotating about a pivot so a small effort over a long arc moves a large resistance over a short arc.

Levers also enable fine control: a long handle on a small cutting edge turns wrist motion into high contact pressure — the functional heart of axes, adzes, and chisels even before the classification is named.

Without explicit lever thinking, tools remain inefficient: short handles waste backs; wrong pivot placement damages edges. Making the lever explicit is prerequisite to later machines built from lever, wheel, and screw families.
overview:
A lever system has a fulcrum, an effort arm, and a load arm. Class I places the fulcrum between load and effort (crowbar). Class II places the load between fulcrum and effort (wheelbarrow conceptually). Class III places effort between fulcrum and load (tongs, fishing rod) — trading force for speed and range.

Mechanical advantage in the ideal model equals the ratio of effort arm to load arm. Real systems lose efficiency to friction at the fulcrum, deformation of the bar, and off-axis loading that introduces bending.

Stone-age fulcrums are rock edges or logs; effort and load contacts are rounded to reduce stress concentrations. Metal ages add hardened pivots and bushings, but geometry remains king.

Levers compose: compound levers in nail clippers and bolt cutters cascade small motions into huge forces. The Codex conserves the root pattern: rigid bar, pivot, two moments in equilibrium (approximately).

principles.name: Torque balance
principles.explanation: Static equilibrium requires the sum of moments about the pivot to be zero — load force times its lever arm equals effort force times its lever arm in the ideal case.

principles.name: Stress and bending
principles.explanation: Long bars in bending fail by outer-fibre tension/compression limits; rectangular sections orient height along bend plane to increase second moment of area.

principles.name: Friction at pivot
principles.explanation: Rough pivots steal mechanical advantage; lubrication (fat, soap later) and hard smooth interfaces reduce parasitic torque.

principles.name: Stability of the fulcrum
principles.explanation: A rolling fulcrum changes effective lever arms during motion — sometimes desirable (cam-like), often hazardous if unplanned.

components.name: Lever beam
components.function: Transmits force between effort and load
components.position: Spans from handle to working end
components.criticalNote: Knots and grain runout weaken bending strength — orient continuous grain along beam

components.name: Fulcrum / pivot
components.position: Under beam at chosen class geometry
components.madeFrom: Hard stone corner, metal pin, roller
components.criticalNote: Contact area too small crushes fibres locally

components.name: Effort interface
components.function: Where human or machine applies input force
components.madeFrom: Shaped wood swell, wrapped cord for grip
components.criticalNote: Handle diameter matches hand — too thin causes nerve compression

components.name: Load interface
components.position: Opposite side of fulcrum from effort depending on class
components.madeFrom: Steel edge, bronze shoe, wood pad
components.criticalNote: Slip at interface converts intended lift into dangerous kick-out

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what The Lever (as a System) is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

rawMaterials.name: Straight-grained hardwood
earthLocations: Oak, ash, hickory in temperate zones
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Season wood; split rather than saw when possible for long fibres

rawMaterials.name: Hard stone fulcrum
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Dress a flat seat to reduce rocking

rawMaterials.name: Lubricants
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Clarify by heating and settling if gritty

buildSteps.title: Define mechanical goal
buildSteps.description: Decide whether you need force multiplication (short load arm) or range of motion (long load arm). Sketch class I/II/III geometry before cutting wood.
buildSteps.warningNote: Wrong class selection cannot be fixed by 'pulling harder'

buildSteps.title: Select and shape the beam
buildSteps.description: Split billet along grain. Taper handle for comfort; leave thickness at maximum bending moment region (often near fulcrum for class I).
buildSteps.warningNote: Shavings ignite easily near fire — clear workspace

buildSteps.title: Seat the fulcrum
buildSteps.description: Carve a shallow saddle into beam or use separate block with anti-slip footing. Test rocking under body weight before applying peak loads.
buildSteps.warningNote: Fulcrum slip under load can throw operator

buildSteps.title: Attach load interface
buildSteps.description: For pry bars, forge or grind a flat claw; for lifting pads, add cross pins to prevent slip. Secure with wedges or metal straps if needed.
buildSteps.warningNote: Pinned joints under tension can eject pins — use shoulders

buildSteps.title: Proof test progressively
buildSteps.description: Load to 1.25× working load with known masses if possible. Listen for fibre cracks. Shorten lever arm if creep appears.
buildSteps.warningNote: Use body posture that keeps load path within base of support — levers amplify kickback too

history.event: Digging sticks, atlatls, and weighted handles embody lever principles long before formal statics.
Archimedes articulates lever law and centre of gravity concepts in Greek mathematical tradition.

inventors.name: Archimedes
inventors.contribution: Mathematical treatment of levers and buoyancy — codified mechanical advantage reasoning

impact: Levers appear in every subsequent machine age: brakes, valve gear, control surfaces, and robotic linkages are torque budgeting problems. Teaching the lever early prevents folk beliefs that 'bigger muscles' are the only answer — geometry is strength.

sources.title: NIST — PML
sources.note: Force metrology for lever and machine experiments

sources.title: Wikipedia — Lever
sources.note: Class I–III levers, torque, and equilibrium

sources.title: OSHA — Heat
sources.note: Industrial hygiene when lifting outdoors in heat (field archaeology parallels)
